sentences.text: I think we were discussing the business of the man getting hit.
That was-- that was actually after-- afterwards.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had-- we had fought for several days in the outpost, and then we pulled back.
And so really, the elements, the-- the severity of the attack by the Germans, had subsided somewhat in our area.
They were still pushing like mad toward Belgium, trying to break through to the sea to split our forces.
But this happened to be-- we were in this-- actually, I was-- our-- our battalion was in corps reserve.
What we were doing was, as I think I earlier mentioned, digging foxholes around the rear to prevent an attack.
If we-- a pending attack would come, we would occupy those holes and fight off any attack by the Germans.

Well this one day-- and I-- I talked a little bit about it-- I was standing there, and this tank across the valley had been shooting rounds from its gun-- a 88-millimeter gun-- into the city of Schonenberg and trying to-- just harassing fire is what it was.
But they saw this-- it happened to be Lloyd [?
Gee ?] was his name-- Sergeant [?
Gee-- ?] was supervising the digging of holes.
And this round hit right next to him and, of course, killed him instantly.
And I went running down there to see what had happened.
And I saw that, of course, he was dead.
[SIGHS] And as I think I mentioned earlier, one of your people is killed, it's like a member of the family being killed.
In the States, this fella and his brother, they were a set of twins.
And oh my gosh, you know, to have a twin killed, the effect on the brother must have been really bad when he found out about it.
And it was-- it was terrible for me and my people, because, like I said, a member of the family.
In the States, it turned out that-- that these two brothers would harassed the dickens out of us officers.
[LAUGHS] You know, they-- they were right on the border of being insubordinate.
And-- but they never were quite.
But they-- they-- they really gave us a hard time, these couple of sergeants.
They were brothers.
And they were pretty feisty.
But in combat, they were-- they were excellent soldiers.
And I felt a great loss and when-- when Lloyd was killed.
And after-- after that episode, we moved back, as I mentioned earlier, moved back to an area.
And that was before we got into-- to this position where I had taken this patrol out, and two of my people were killed.
We were in a place near Hagenow, which is in Alsace-Lorraine.
Hagenow and Schweighausen were two of the cities that were in the-- near the Black Forest.
And we did a lot of fighting in the forest.
And as a matter of fact, I learned early on that if you're in a forest and you-- some artillery rounds are coming into that forest.
And they're bursting in the trees above you, that you don't fall on the ground.
You stand behind a tree.
And I taught my guys to do that.
Don't ever hit the dirt when you hear an artillery round coming, because they whine and then they hit in the top of the trees.
And if you're lying on the ground, you're going to get hurt.
So you try to figure out from what direction this round is coming, and you go to the opposite side of the tree.
And unless it hits in the top of this tree that you're behind, you're-- you're probably going to survive.
And so there was a lot of skirmishes in this area, in Hagenow and Schweighausen and some of those small towns.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- as a matter of fact, in this area, we had-- we had been forced back again.
We were in an outpost line across this open valley.
And we pulled back after we were contacted by the Germans, pulled back across this valley.
And the regimental commander wanted us to go back out and re-assume the-- the positions we were in.
And our company commander fought this, because he said they're already there.
He said, I don't care.
You send your troops back out there and retake that position.
This was Colonel Luongo.
Well, we went-- we almost got back to where the positions were.
And I was out in the front of my platoon.
And I looked up, and here's some binoculars looking at me.
And so I said, Krauts!
And we spread out.
And I-- I ended up in a mine field-- anti-tank minefield.
And the people back on the main line saw what was going on, so they called in artillery to fire on the Germans that were right there and right out in front of me, not 50 yards.
And some of this artillery was-- was landing in the minefield that I was lying in.
I could look up, and I could see-- [LAUGHS] see Germans, you know.
And I-- I tried to-- to make myself as small as possible.
Finally, when I saw most of my guys had gotten back, I crawled back and was able to get out of there.
And the people in my company assumed I was dead.
And so I came back, and I received a welcoming [LAUGHS] committee when I got back.
Boy, that was-- that was scary.
That was really scary.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Really.
But-- so we-- we assumed a move then to this area that we occupied from latter part of February to when we assumed the-- the attack in March.

And so from the-- which I described before, this business of-- of being pinned down on the side of the hill and-- and running down and capturing these Germans-- we went on and lost-- lost these folks from snipers and neutralizing the snipers, and then went on toward the Siegfried Line.
And we ran into a lot of-- lot of small battles along the way.
One in particular, we were heading down this, I guess you call it a saddle.
It was-- it was a U-shaped junction, I guess, of a valley.
And we were heading down there.
And two tanks across the valley started firing their guns at us.
And-- and the sound of an 88 is really traumatic, because you hear the-- you hear the-- if you-- if you aren't hit by one, you hear it bang over your head.
And then you hear the gun going off.
They're so fast, just several thousand feet per second those rounds go.
And they're that big around, you know?
And so we-- we got as low to the ground as we could get and and survived most of that.
And the tanks pulled away, and we continued on with our chasing the Germans across Alsace-Lorraine.
And got to Dahn, I guess it was, D-A-H-N, the name of a small town which was some 10 miles from the Siegfried Line.
And we kind of bivouacked there and-- and got refurbished with ammunition and food.
And some of us were even able to take showers.
And we then took off for the Maginot Line.
Not at-- not at that time, not until we actually got into Germany did we do it.
Well, we captured some-- a dozen or so-- in that skirmish that I talked about.
They were sent to the rear.
We sent a guard with them to-- for rear echelon units to pick them up and guard them until they were able to transfer them back to a-- a POW compound in the rear of our units.
We were then-- at Dahn, got-- got our-- our ammunition, got cleaned up.
And we were transported by truck then to cross the Rhine River at Worms, W-O-R-M-S, Worms.
And that's when we re-entered combat, which-- our first big combat then after being transported.
On the other side of the Rhine River was Wurzburg.
And Wurzburg was a heavy fight.
We-- we just fought all day and all night.
And-- and we were fighting a pretty elite German Mountain Division, elements of that Mountain Division in Wurzburg.
And-- finally, we were able to-- we captured a number of Germans during that skirmish in Wurzbur.
And it was kind of a-- a funny situation there.
Wurzburg, there's a big castle or-- or stronghold fort up above the city.
You-- you may be familiar with Wurzburg.
It's called a festung.
And a great big wall that surrounded this fort-- fortress, and on it was 'heil Hitler' was written on this wall.
By the time we left, 'heil Hitler' was erased, and there was a rainbow on it.
We painted rainbows on everything.
Wherever we'd go, there'd be a rainbow when we left.
There must have been 1,000 rainbows across Alsace-Lorraine and-- and into Germany and across Germany.
And so we pushed on then after our fight in Wurzburg.
We lost, oh, three or four people in Wurzburg, because we were-- that was-- that was pretty heavy combat.
The next real heavy combat was in Furth-Nuremberg.
We-- our-- my particular company's objective was the airport at Furth.
And we had quite a battle there.
Although I-- in retrospect, I felt quite sorry for some of the folks we killed.
They were-- they were air-- air force cadets.
We didn't know it at the time, but it turned out that they were air force cadets.
And one of the battalion commanders, or the commander of this unit-- school, I guess it was, at the Furth airport-- was a-- a a colonel, oberst.
I guess it was oberstleutnant, lieutenant colonel.
We killed him.
And it turned out that he was the commandant of the school, and oh-- [SIGHS] You know, at the one time, here's an enemy.
But they're-- they're cadets.
They're young kids.
And here's that colonel that probably was over age.
And he was killed, too.
And-- and you even felt sorry for those folks in the heat of combat.
But they had weapons.
And so unfortunately, we had to-- had to try to-- try to-- to kill them.
Kill or be killed was the motto in those days.
And then we-- we moved from the airport across the river into Nuremberg.
And we had city fighting then, heavy city fighting.
We fought for two days in Nuremberg.
And all of a sudden one morning, about 6:00 AM, the whole garrison-- German garrison-- came marching down the street.
And we ordered them to pile their weapons in the square in Nuremberg.
There were 2,500 Germans that we captured all at once that morning.
And they just gave up the garrison.
Again, we had guards from our unit guard them until the military police and rear echelon units could to take care of them and march them back to the rear.
So we had a-- a big column of-- of German POWs being marched back to the rear.
And they-- they were taken back and put into a POW camp in-- much to the rear of our units.
Furth airport?
Any American prisoners?
No.
Prisoners that were held by the Germans.
No, no.
We ran into the-- this-- these-- this school, Air Force Luftwaffe school, where the bunch of cadets and-- and a few instructors.
And we fought a bit there.
But it was no contest.
We were heavily armed and they weren't.
And that-- that kind of has haunted me for-- ever since, that we were killing-- not innocents-- but killing kids.
And same thing happened in the Siegfried Line, and I'll get into that.
So then, after having captured Furth and-- and captured Nuremberg, we then went to-- let's see, where we go from there?
We went toward Munich.
But on the way, we had to cross the Danube River.
And that was an interesting experience for sure.
The engineers had built a-- a bridge across the river.
And we, early one morning, got there.
And we went across the river.
And we were moving across this wide-open plain toward Donauworth, which is a-- a city on the river.
And halfway across this-- it had to be two miles across at least-- halfway across, we came under timed fire artillery.
The bursts were bursting over our heads.
And I yelled at my guys, don't hit the dirt.
Run!
Run!

And we ran probably a mile and a half, the reason being that it was timed fire, and if we kept running, then they couldn't correct their-- their-- the distance that they were to fire to get the explosions to go off over our heads as easy as if we'd have sat there.
They'd have had us all-- all dead if we just sat there.
So we ran and ran and ran and finally got to the other side of this open plain and came upon this German unit that was in their foxholes on-- on the other side of a-- of a valley.
And so we fought them for a long time.
And that's when I became the [? company ?] commander.
Our-- our commander, who was then a lieutenant, Lieutenant Powers, was hit by one of our own tanks.
[LAUGHS] Hit the tree that he was under, and he got shrapnel in his back.
So he-- he said, Dunn, I need you.
And so I came up to where he was.
And he had been hurt pretty bad.
And he said, you've got to take over the company.
So he was taken back to the rear to the hospital.
And I took over the company then.
And we proceeded on to-- to the fight several small skirmishes along the way.
Did he survive?
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, we-- we visited him in Massachusetts a few years ago.
He-- he since passed away, but he survived.
And he-- he was a crazy guy.
Every town we got into, he'd look for a woman [LAUGHS] if he could possibly find one.
And he-- he made out pretty well, I guess.
One story he likes-- he-- he told was, we were coming upon this small city, a small village down the hill.
It was probably, oh, five miles down in front of us.
And we took it under fire.
And then when we didn't get any-- finally didn't get any return fire, we went down into the town to ferret out any-- anybody holed up in the town.
And he and I-- he was the first platoon leader at the time-- and I had been very close.
And we supported one another.
And we would look on the opposite side of the street.
And he'd look on the opposite side of the street and make sure that there wasn't somebody with a gun pointing at us.
And-- and we came to this first-- the first set of buildings.
And he said, I saw you and one of your guys throw open the door and run in.
And you came out with this horrified look on your face.
And he said, I didn't know what had happened.
And so he said, you told me later that-- I said, what did you see in there?
And I said, there was a lady getting dressed.
[LAUGHS]
And you know, I'm-- I-- I was so naive in those days that seeing a lady in half-- half-dressed was-- was pretty traumatic then.
[LAUGHS] I'm a 20-year-old naive lieutenant with hardened combat experience, but I was not prepared for that.
[LAUGHS] So he said, you looked-- you looked absolutely frightened out of your gourd!
[LAUGHS] And so he keeps-- every time we'd get together, he'd remind me of that experience.
So then we were-- after having gotten to-- through these series of-- of combat situations heading toward Munich, we happened to be just-- just north, probably 40 miles, north of Munich.
And this one night-- it was the 28th of April, as a matter of fact, in '45.
That night, I was called to the battalion headquarters.
Already had orders to-- to attack Munich and-- and capture the portion that we were assigned.
The battalion commander said, our orders have changed.
He said, you've got to go to Dachau-- the city of Dachau.
There's a concentration camp there that-- that we are to attack and take over.
And I asked, what is a concentration camp?
And he didn't know.
I thought, well, it's a what?
A POW camp?
A few hundred prisoners of war there?
And he wasn't sure.
But it was mandatory that we get there in all haste.
And so we jumped on tanks and half-tracks, and Jeeps and trucks and took off and got there early in the morning of the 29th.
And we went through the city of Dachau and got to-- to this concentration camp on the outskirts of Dachau.
And the 45th-- the elements of the 45th Division had gotten there a couple of hours before we did.
And they captured all the-- all the guards and other military-- German military that was in that area.
And I learned later that some of those that weren't killed were executed, which some people took as an atrocity, that they gave up and we executed them, or this division did.
Yeah, they were lined up against a wall and executed summarily.
And I'll talk about this a little later, associated with the Holocaust commemoration, where I met some of the people from the 45th Division who were-- were invited because of the liberation.
So we got to this concentration camp, and we-- we didn't meet any resistance, and I didn't know why.
Why didn't we get fired upon as we came toward the camp?
Well, it was because, as I said, the elements had gotten there before us and had killed all the-- the combatants.
And our-- my job-- I was informed by the battalion commander-- my job was to take my company into the camp and guard it.
And I felt rather strange with a-- a-- an order like that.
We're supposed to be liberating these-- the-- the prisoners of war that are in this camp, right?
So we-- we broke down the-- the entrance to the camp and went in and could not believe what we saw-- thousands and thousands of people, emaciated, stacks of bodies who had recently been killed.
And I'll tell you why that happened a few minutes.
I-- I met a Belgian officer who had been captured a few months earlier.
And he spoke good English.
And I said, what is going on here?
And I don't know quite what the emotions were, but revulsion, I guess, is one.
Stark shock is another.
How could this be?
How could so many people be in this condition?
Because we had no concept of what a concentration camp was.
But I guess I should have known that it wasn't a POW camp because of the size of it, the walls around it, and the barbed wire, and the-- and the-- the towers, and all that.
I-- I-- I guess I-- I thought that, well, it's something different than-- than what I expected then.
But I'd had no idea going into this camp what I was going to see, nor did my guys.
Some of us-- I didn't get sick enough to vomit, but I got sick to my stomach, of course.
And a number of them just threw up, seeing this horrific situation.

And then I-- this Belgian officer and some of the other people that-- that were actually quartered in-- near this entrance that we went through, they weren't in the main barracks with the rest of the internees.
They were treated a little more humanely, I guess.
And I guess so, because he was-- he was not emaciated like the other-- most of the other prisoners.
So he took me into some of the barracks to show-- show me what-- what it was like in there.
It was just awful, just awful.
And the-- the looked like cordwood, bodies lying around the camp everywhere.
Went to the crematorium, and it was stacked to the ceiling with bodies that hadn't been cremated.
Let's stop here for a minute, because I want you to describe this whole scene on the next tape.
We have to change tapes.
All right.
